The **grey-headed flying fox** (***Pteropus poliocephalus***) is a [megabat](/wiki/Megabat "Megabat") native to [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").[[3]](#cite_note-MSW3-3) The species shares mainland Australia with three other members of the [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") *[Pteropus](/wiki/Pteropus "Pteropus")*: the little red *[P. scapulatus](/wiki/Pteropus_scapulatus "Pteropus scapulatus")*, spectacled *[P. conspicillatus](/wiki/Pteropus_conspicillatus "Pteropus conspicillatus")*, and the black *[P. alecto](/wiki/Pteropus_alecto "Pteropus alecto")*. The grey-headed flying fox is the largest bat in Australia.

The grey-headed flying fox is endemic to the south-eastern forested areas of Australia, principally east of the [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range"). Its range extends approximately from [Bundaberg](/wiki/Bundaberg,_Queensland "Bundaberg, Queensland") in Queensland to [Geelong](/wiki/Geelong,_Victoria "Geelong, Victoria") in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"), with outlying colonies in [Ingham](/wiki/Ingham,_Queensland "Ingham, Queensland") and [Finch Hatton](/wiki/Finch_Hatton,_Queensland "Finch Hatton, Queensland") in the north, and in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide,_South_Australia "Adelaide, South Australia") in the south. In the southern parts of its range it occupies more extreme latitudes than any other *Pteropus* species.

As of 2021, the species is listed as "[Vulnerable](/wiki/Vulnerable_species "Vulnerable species")" on the [IUCN Red List of Threatened Species](/wiki/IUCN_Red_List "IUCN Red List").[[1]](#cite_note-iucn-1)

## Taxonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Taxonomy")]

A description of the species was published by [Coenraad Temminck](/wiki/Coenraad_Jacob_Temminck "Coenraad Jacob Temminck") in his 1825 monograph of mammals. Hybridisation with the species *[Pteropus alecto](/wiki/Pteropus_alecto "Pteropus alecto")* has been noted where their ranges intersect.[[3]](#cite_note-MSW3-3)

The common names for *Pteropus poliocephalus* include grey-headed kalong.[[4]](#cite_note-RCS1859-4) The entry in Gould's *[Mammals of Australia](/wiki/The_Mammals_of_Australia "The Mammals of Australia")* (1863) gave the bat the title grey-headed vampire.[[5]](#cite_note-Gould1863-5)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Grey_headed_flying_fox_-_skimming_water_-_AndrewMercer_-_DSC00530.jpg/250px-Grey_headed_flying_fox_-_skimming_water_-_AndrewMercer_-_DSC00530.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grey_headed_flying_fox_-_skimming_water_-_AndrewMercer_-_DSC00530.jpg)

Wingspan is visible as this flying fox skims water to drink.

The grey-headed flying fox is the largest bat in Australia, with the adult wingspan reaching up to 1 m (3 ft 3 in) in length and weighing up to 1 kg (2.2 lb).[[6]](#cite_note-nsw-6) Weight generally varies between 600 and 1,000 g (21 and 35 oz), with an average of 700 g (25 oz). The combined length of the head and body is from 230 to 290 mm. The forearm length is a range from 138 to 180 mm. The length of the ear from the tip to base is 30 to 37 mm.[[7]](#cite_note-Menkhorst2011-7)

The overall colour of the [pelage](/wiki/Pelage "Pelage") is a dark-grey body with a light-grey head, separated by a reddish-brown collar.
The fur on the body is long and streaked with grey, the broad and well defined collar completely encircles the neck with hair that is golden orange in tone. A unique characteristic among bats of the genus *Pteropus* is fur on the legs that extends all the way to the ankle.[[7]](#cite_note-Menkhorst2011-7)

Like many [megachiropterans](/wiki/Megachiroptera "Megachiroptera"), the species lacks a tail. All of these bats possess claws on its first and second digits.
The head is simple in form, with the characteristic 'dog-like' appearance of the genus.
Since it does not [echolocate](/wiki/Animal_echolocation#Bats "Animal echolocation"), it lacks the [tragus](/wiki/Tragus_(ear) "Tragus (ear)") or [leaf ornamentation](/wiki/Nose-leaf "Nose-leaf") found in many species of [Microchiroptera](/wiki/Microchiroptera "Microchiroptera").
It relies on smell and, predominately, sight to locate its [food](/wiki/Food "Food") (nectar, pollen and native fruits) and thus has relatively large eyes for a bat.

The voice of *P. poliocephalus* consists of a complex series of squeals and screechings.[[7]](#cite_note-Menkhorst2011-7) They will flap their wings in hot weather, using blood pumped through the [patagium](/wiki/Patagium "Patagium") to cool the body temperature.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards201250-8)

The grey-headed flying fox is long-lived for a mammal of its size. Individuals reportedly survived in captivity for up to 23 years, and a maximum age of up to 15 years seems possible in the wild.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Grey-headed flying foxes have been found to experience [torpor](/wiki/Torpor "Torpor").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ecology")]

### Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Distribution")]

The distribution range is at the eastern regions of the Australian continent, mostly within 200 kilometres of the coast, from [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone,_Queensland "Gladstone, Queensland") in Queensland through to the southern [Gippsland](/wiki/Gippsland "Gippsland") region and populations around the city of [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). The breeding range has been recorded as progressing southward, the temperate climate of Melbourne and [Geelong](/wiki/Geelong "Geelong") and no further north than [Maryborough, Queensland](/wiki/Maryborough,_Queensland "Maryborough, Queensland").[[7]](#cite_note-Menkhorst2011-7)

[Urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanisation "Urbanisation") may displace the species, or provide habitat that accommodates their feeding or roosting preferences. The city of Brisbane has many roosts occupied by the species; a famous colony at the [Indooroopilly Island](/wiki/Indooroopilly_Island "Indooroopilly Island") is noted for the evening departure of the bats across the local river. Within the central business district of Sydney, they can be seen travelling along city streets to feed at [Moreton Bay fig](/wiki/Moreton_Bay_fig "Moreton Bay fig") trees at [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards201228–30-10) The species was recorded as an occasional visitor to the national capital Canberra, although the flowering eucalypts at [Commonwealth Park](/wiki/Commonwealth_Park "Commonwealth Park") have seen more permanent camps established close to the city.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards201233-11)

The species was surveyed during the 1920s by [Francis Ratcliffe](/wiki/Francis_Ratcliffe "Francis Ratcliffe"), who recorded the populations in estimates of quarter, half, or one million in camps, generally located around 40 kilometres apart. These numbers have greatly declined since this first survey.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012107-12)

### Habitat and movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Habitat and movements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Pteropus_poliocephalus_colony.JPG/250px-Pteropus_poliocephalus_colony.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pteropus_poliocephalus_colony.JPG)

Grey-headed flying fox colony

Grey-headed flying foxes live in a variety of habitats, including [rainforests](/wiki/Rainforests "Rainforests"), woodlands, and swamps.[[13]](#cite_note-Menkhorst_1995-13) These camps are variable in size and are seasonally relocated; the warmer parts of the year find them occupying cool and wet gullies in large groups.[[7]](#cite_note-Menkhorst2011-7) During the day, individuals reside in large roosts (colonies or 'camps') consisting of hundreds to tens of thousands of individuals. Colonies are formed in seemingly arbitrary locations. Roost vegetation includes rainforest patches, stands of [melaleuca](/wiki/Melaleuca "Melaleuca"), [mangroves](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove"), and [riparian vegetation](/wiki/Riparian_vegetation "Riparian vegetation"), but roosts also occupy highly modified vegetation in urban areas. A prominent example existed for many years at the [Royal Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Royal_Botanic_Gardens,_Sydney "Royal Botanic Gardens, Sydney") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"). However, the botanic gardens instituted a controversial policy to remove them from the garden grounds. The camp is now dispersed across Queensland.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Movements of grey-headed flying foxes are influenced by the availability of food. Their population is very fluid, as they move in response to the irregular blossoming of certain plant species. They are [keystone](/wiki/Keystone_species "Keystone species") pollinators and seed dispersers of over 100 species of native trees and plants.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The grey-headed flying fox is a partial migrant that uses winds to facilitate long-distance movement. It does not migrate in a constant direction, but rather in the direction that will be the most beneficial at the time.[[17]](#cite_note-Tidemann_2004-17)

Although recorded in small numbers sporadically throughout the 20th century, it was not until the 1980s that grey-headed flying foxes routinely visited Melbourne,[[18]](#cite_note-pcb-18) with a permanent camp since the 1990s. Their residence at the [Royal Botanic Gardens Victoria](/wiki/Royal_Botanic_Gardens_Victoria "Royal Botanic Gardens Victoria") was the subject of controversy, and the bats were eventually discouraged and moved to [Yarra Bend](/wiki/Yarra_Bend "Yarra Bend") at the city's river. The camp at this site was decimated during a heat wave, requiring its rehabilitation to sustain the relocated population. The forced relocations are also said to have led to the discovery of the orchards of the [Goulburn Valley](/wiki/Goulburn_Valley "Goulburn Valley").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards201234–35-19) Similarly, the first recorded permanent camp in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide") was established in 2010. The spread is likely due to [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"), habitat loss and drought; while the location of the new camps appears to be in response to [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanisation "Urbanisation"): a reliable food supply[[20]](#cite_note-mtlofty-20) (such as native eucalypt plantings and backyard fruit trees) and warmer temperatures due to climate change and [urban heat islands](/wiki/Urban_heat_islands "Urban heat islands").[[18]](#cite_note-pcb-18)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012118-21)

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit), the bats have been spreading westward, with camps spotted in [Port Augusta](/wiki/Port_Augusta "Port Augusta"), on the [Eyre Peninsula](/wiki/Eyre_Peninsula "Eyre Peninsula").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Diet and foraging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Diet and foraging")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Grey-headed_Flying_Fox_%28IMG0526%29.jpg/250px-Grey-headed_Flying_Fox_%28IMG0526%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grey-headed_Flying_Fox_(IMG0526).jpg)

Feeding on plant nectar.

Around dusk, grey-headed flying foxes leave the roost and travel up to 50 km a night to feed on pollen, [nectar](/wiki/Nectar "Nectar") and fruit.[[17]](#cite_note-Tidemann_2004-17) The species consumes fruit flowers and pollens of around 187 plant species.[[23]](#cite_note-23) These include [eucalypt](/wiki/Eucalypt "Eucalypt"), particularly *[Corymbia gummifera](/wiki/Corymbia_gummifera "Corymbia gummifera")*, *[Eucalyptus muelleriana](/wiki/Eucalyptus_muelleriana "Eucalyptus muelleriana")*, *[E. globoidea](/wiki/Eucalyptus_globoidea "Eucalyptus globoidea")* and *[E. botryoides](/wiki/Eucalyptus_botryoides "Eucalyptus botryoides")*,[[13]](#cite_note-Menkhorst_1995-13)[[24]](#cite_note-Parry-Jones_2001-24) and fruits from a wide range of rainforest trees, including members of the genus *[Ficus](/wiki/Ficus "Ficus")*.[[13]](#cite_note-Menkhorst_1995-13)[[24]](#cite_note-Parry-Jones_2001-24) These bats are considered sequential specialists, since they feed on a variety of foods.[[24]](#cite_note-Parry-Jones_2001-24) Grey-headed flying foxes, along with the three other Australian flying fox species, fulfill a very important ecological role by dispersing the pollen and seeds of a wide range of native Australian plants. The grey-headed flying fox is the only mammalian [nectarivore](/wiki/Nectarivore "Nectarivore") and [frugivore](/wiki/Frugivore "Frugivore") to occupy substantial areas of subtropical rainforests, so is of key importance to those forests.

The teeth, tongue and palate of the [pteropodid](/wiki/Pteropodidae "Pteropodidae") bats are able to extract plant juices from food, only swallowing smaller seeds of the meal. Incisors hold items such as fruit, and the fibrous material is ejected from the mouth after it is masticated and the juice is swallowed; larger seeds may be held in the mouth and dispersed several kilometres from the tree. The need for the elaborate intestinal tract of most herbivores is consequently removed. Some fruiting plants produce food for flying-foxes, and *P. poliocephalus* is attracted to the scent of their flowers and fruit and is able to locate the pale colour that indicates the source; the fruit and blooms of species that attract birds in the daylight are usually contrasting reds and purples. The food source is also presented away from the foliage that may obstruct the bat's access.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards201270-25)

Most of the trees on which this species forages produce nectar and pollen seasonally and are abundant unpredictably, so the flying fox's migration traits cope with this. The time when flying foxes leave their roosts to feed depends on foraging light and predation risk. Flying foxes have more time and light when foraging if they leave their roosts early in the day. The entire colony may leave later if a predatory bird is present, while lactating females leave earlier. With males, the bachelors leave earlier than harem-holding males, which guard and wait until all their females have left. The flying foxes that leave the roost earlier are more vulnerable to predation, and some flying foxes will wait for others to leave, a phenomenon labelled the "after you" effect.[[26]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2006-26)

### Social organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Social organisation")]

#### Groupings and territories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Groupings and territories")]

Grey-headed flying foxes form two different roosting camps, summer camps and winter camps.[[27]](#cite_note-Nelson_1965-27) Summer camps are used from September to April or June. In these camps, they establish [territories](/wiki/Territory_(animal) "Territory (animal)"), [mate](/wiki/Mating "Mating"), and reproduce.[[27]](#cite_note-Nelson_1965-27) Winter camps are used from April to September. The sexes are separated in winter camps and most behaviour is characterised by mutual grooming.[[27]](#cite_note-Nelson_1965-27) Summer camps are considered "main camps", while winter camps are referred to as "transit camps".[[27]](#cite_note-Nelson_1965-27)

In their summer camps, starting in January, male grey-headed flying foxes set up mating territories. Mating territories are generally 3.5 body lengths along branches.[[28]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2005-28) These flying foxes' neck glands enlarge in males in the mating season, and are used to mark the territories.[[28]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2005-28) The males fight to maintain their territories, and this is associated with a steep drop in the males' body condition during this time.[[29]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2011-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2010-30)
Around the beginning of the mating season, adult females move from the periphery towards the central male territories where they become part of short-term 'harems' that consist of a male and an unstable group of up to five females.[[28]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2005-28) Centrally located males are polygamous, while males on the periphery are monogamous or single.[[27]](#cite_note-Nelson_1965-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2005-28) The mating system of the grey-headed flying fox is best described as a [lek](/wiki/Lek_(biology) "Lek (biology)") because males do not provide any essential resources to females and are chosen on the basis of their physical location within the roost, which correlates with male quality.[[28]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2005-28)

#### Reproduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Reproduction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Grey_headed_flying_fox_with_pup_-_AndrewMercer_-_DSC10055.jpg/250px-Grey_headed_flying_fox_with_pup_-_AndrewMercer_-_DSC10055.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grey_headed_flying_fox_with_pup_-_AndrewMercer_-_DSC10055.jpg)

Mother with near-mature pup

Matings are generally observed between March and May, but the most likely time of conception is April.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Most mating takes place in the territories and during the day. Females have control over the copulation process, and males may have to keep mating with the same females.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Females usually give birth to one young each year.[[33]](#cite_note-Richards2000-33) [Gestation](/wiki/Gestation "Gestation") lasts around 27 weeks,[[34]](#cite_note-34) and pregnant females give birth between late September and November. Late births into January are sometimes observed. The [altricial](/wiki/Altricial "Altricial") newborns rely on their mothers for warmth.[[35]](#cite_note-35) For their first three weeks, young cling to their mothers when they go foraging. After this, the young remain in the roosts. By January, young are capable of sustained flight, and by February, March or April are fully weaned.

### Predation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Predation")]

Flying foxes are preyed on by [eagles](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle"), [goannas](/wiki/Goanna "Goanna") and [snakes](/wiki/Snake "Snake").[[27]](#cite_note-Nelson_1965-27)

The camps of *P. poliocephalus* attract a number of larger predators. including both terrestrial and aerial hunters.
The sea eagle *[Haliaeetus leucogaster](/wiki/Haliaeetus_leucogaster "Haliaeetus leucogaster")* will capture these bats in flight as they leave their roosts.
The snake species *[Morelia spilota](/wiki/Morelia_spilota "Morelia spilota")* is frequently found as a resident at these camps, lazily selecting an individual from the apparently unconcerned group at a branch. The bat is seized in the jaws and encircled by the python's body, then swallowed head first to be digested over the next week.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012104–105-36)
The species was reported by [John Gould](/wiki/John_Gould "John Gould") as being eaten by [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians").[[5]](#cite_note-Gould1863-5)

## Conservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Conservation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Electrocuted_Flying_Fox.jpg/250px-Electrocuted_Flying_Fox.jpg)](/wiki/File:Electrocuted_Flying_Fox.jpg)

A grey-headed flying fox electrocuted between electricity transmission lines in suburban Sydney

The grey-headed flying fox is now a prominent federal conservation problem in Australia. Early in the last century, the species was considered abundant, with numbers estimated in the many millions. In recent years, though, evidence has been accumulating that the species is in serious decline. An estimate for the species in 2019 put the number at 586,000[[37]](#cite_note-37) and the national population may have declined by over 30% between 1989 and 1999 alone.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Threats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Threats")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Male_grey-headed_flying_fox%2C_suffering_from_heat_stress.jpg/250px-Male_grey-headed_flying_fox%2C_suffering_from_heat_stress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Male_grey-headed_flying_fox,_suffering_from_heat_stress.jpg)

A male grey-headed flying fox, suffering from heat stress during a heatwave in New South Wales

Grey-headed flying foxes are exposed to several threats, including loss of foraging and roosting habitat,[[39]](#cite_note-39) competition with the black flying fox, and mass die-offs caused by extreme temperature events.[[28]](#cite_note-Welbergen_2005-28) When present in urban environments, grey-headed flying foxes are sometimes perceived as a nuisance. Cultivated orchard fruits are also taken, but apparently only at times when other food items are scarce. Because their roosting and foraging habits bring the species into conflict with humans, they suffer from direct killing of animals in orchards and harassment and destruction of roosts. Negative public perception of the species has intensified with the discovery of three recently emerged [zoonotic](/wiki/Zoonosis "Zoonosis") [viruses](/wiki/Virus "Virus") that are potentially fatal to humans: [Hendra virus](/wiki/Hendra_virus "Hendra virus"), [Australian bat lyssavirus](/wiki/Australian_bat_lyssavirus "Australian bat lyssavirus") and [Menangle virus](/wiki/Menangle_virus "Menangle virus").[[40]](#cite_note-40) However, only Australian bat lyssavirus is known from two isolated cases to be directly transmissible from bats to humans. No person has ever died from ABLV (Lyssavirus) after having had the ABLV post-exposure vaccine.[[41]](#cite_note-pope-41)

The urbanised camps of cities were noted as succumbing to poisoning during the 1970s to 1980s, identified as the [lead in petrol](/wiki/Tetraethyl_lead "Tetraethyl lead") that would accumulate on the fur and enter the body when grooming. The mortality rate from toxic levels of lead in the environment dropped with the introduction of unleaded fuel in 1985.
An introduced plant, the cocos palm *[Syagrus romanzoffiana](/wiki/Syagrus_romanzoffiana "Syagrus romanzoffiana")*, now banned by some local councils, bears fruit that is toxic to this species and has resulted in their death; the Chinese elm *[Ulmus parvifolia](/wiki/Ulmus_parvifolia "Ulmus parvifolia")* and [privet](/wiki/Privet "Privet") present this same hazard. The species is vulnerable to diseases that may kill large numbers within a camp, and the sudden incidence of premature births in colonies is likely to significantly impact the re-population of the group; the cause of these disorders or diseases is unknown.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012107–109-42)

Recent research has shown, since 1994, more than 24,500 grey-headed flying foxes have died from extreme heat events alone.[[43]](#cite_note-Welbergen_et_al._2008-43)
Unsuitable backyard fruit tree netting also kills many animals and may bring them into close contact with humans, but can be avoided by using wildlife-safe netting. [Barbed wire](/wiki/Barbed_wire "Barbed wire") accounts for many casualties; this can be ameliorated by removing old or unnecessary barbed wire or marking it with bright paint.[[41]](#cite_note-pope-41)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012107-12)

The early twentieth century saw the incursion of *Pteropus poliocephalus* to the opportunities they discovered at orchards, and the government placed a bounty on the declared pest.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012107–109-42) Their reputation for destroying fruit crops was noted by John Gould in 1863,[[5]](#cite_note-Gould1863-5) though the extent of actual damage was often greatly exaggerated. When Ratcliffe submitted his report, he noted the number of paid bounties was 300,000, and this would not have included the mortally wounded escapees or those left suspended at roosts by the grip that is held by their weight. This species continued to be killed or wounded by shotguns, many remaining disabled where they fell after the bounty was stopped, despite the advice of Ratcliffe and later researchers on an ineffective and uneconomical practice and the needless extermination of the population. Orchardists have begun shifting to the use of netting that also discourages the daytime visits of birds. The impact of indiscriminate shooting of bats has resulted in the species being declared vulnerable to extinction, to the tree species that relied on them for regeneration, the subsequent alteration to the forest ecology of the [eastern states](/wiki/Eastern_states_of_Australia "Eastern states of Australia")[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2012107–109-42)

### Status and protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Status and protection")]

To answer some of the growing threats, roost sites have been legally protected since 1986 in New South Wales and since 1994 in Queensland. In 1999, the species was classified as "Vulnerable to extinction" in The Action Plan for Australian Bats,[[44]](#cite_note-44) and has since been protected across its range under Australian federal law, listed as Vulnerable under the *[Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999](/wiki/Environment_Protection_and_Biodiversity_Conservation_Act_1999 "Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999")* (EPBC Act). A [species recovery plan](/wiki/Species_recovery_plan "Species recovery plan") was created by the federal [Department of Agriculture, Water and the Environment](/wiki/Department_of_Agriculture,_Water_and_the_Environment "Department of Agriculture, Water and the Environment") and the South Australian [Department for Environment and Water](/wiki/Department_for_Environment_and_Water "Department for Environment and Water") and published in 2021.[[45]](#cite_note-sprat-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit) the species is listed as "Vulnerable" on the [IUCN Red List of Threatened Species](/wiki/IUCN_Red_List_of_Threatened_Species "IUCN Red List of Threatened Species") under criteria A2ace and A4ac. Justification for the assessment says that "although the population is relatively large (exceeding 10,000 mature individuals) and it has a large extent of occurrence (> 20,000 km²), a continuing population decline is inferred to be more than 30–35% over the last three generations", and that further decline is expected.[[1]](#cite_note-iucn-1)

### Wildlife rescue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grey-headed_flying_fox&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Wildlife rescue")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Greyheadedflyingfoxbabies2008canungra.jpg/250px-Greyheadedflyingfoxbabies2008canungra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greyheadedflyingfoxbabies2008canungra.jpg)

Crib full of abandoned babies rescued by Wildcare Australia in care at The Bat Hospital

Baby flying foxes usually come into care after having been separated from their mothers. Babies are often orphaned during four to six weeks of age, when they inadvertently fall off their mothers during flight, often due to disease or tick paralysis (their own and/or that of the mother).[[47]](#cite_note-Dovas-47)

Bat caregivers are not only specially trained in techniques to rescue and rehabilitate bats, but they are also vaccinated against [rabies](/wiki/Rabies "Rabies"). Although the chance of contracting the rabies-like Australian bat [lyssavirus](/wiki/Lyssavirus "Lyssavirus") is extremely small, bat caregivers are inoculated for their own protection.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
